step_by_step_thinking:
The most suitable statistical test for comparing the mean height differences across three socioeconomic groups would be one-way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA). This is because it assesses whether there are any statistically significant differences between two or more means from different independent groups. In contrast, t-tests compare only two sample means while chi-square tests evaluate categorical data associations rather than continuous variables such as height measurements.
\boxed{D} Based on the context given about testing differences in heights among multiple socio-economic groups, we need an omnibus test capable of handling comparisons beyond just pairwise contrasts - making one-way ANOVA the best fit here.
\boxed{D} Given the task involves assessing differences in a continuous variable (heights) across >2 unrelated groups (socioeconomic status categories), one-way ANOVA is indeed the correct choice over other options listed. It evaluates overall mean differences without needing pre-specification of all pairs being compared.
raw_response:

This is because it assesses whether there are any statistically significant differences between two or more means from different independent groups. In contrast, t-tests compare only two sample means while chi-square tests evaluate categorical data associations rather than continuous variables such as height measurements.
\boxed{D} Based on the context given about testing differences in heights among multiple socio-economic groups, we need an omnibus test capable of handling comparisons beyond just pairwise contrasts - making one-way ANOVA the best fit here.
\boxed{D} Given the task involves assessing differences in a continuous variable (heights) across >2 unrelated groups (socioeconomic status categories), one-way ANOVA is indeed the correct choice over other options listed. It evaluates overall mean differences without needing pre-specification of all pairs being compared.
\boxed{D}
